title: Investor Overview PDF
subtitle: AI checkpoint infrastructure for regulated workflows.
shortDescription: Market timing, product path, commercial route and expansion logic for Corentis.
evidenceHook: Frames Corentis at the intersection of regulated-service pressure and rapid AI adoption.
ctaText: Request an investor or design partner conversation.
disclaimer: This document is for discussion and pilot exploration only. It does not constitute legal, regulatory, investment or compliance advice.

sections.heading: Opening position
sections.body: Corentis Shield is an AI checkpoint for regulated workflows. It is designed to sit between AI agents and regulated actions, checking proposed outputs against policy, risk rules, human-review requirements and evidence requirements before anything sensitive moves forward.

sections.heading: The core problem
sections.body: AI adoption is accelerating, but regulated teams cannot rely on AI agents in sensitive workflows unless actions are controlled, reviewed and evidenced. The risk is not only that AI may produce a weak answer. The risk grows when that answer becomes a customer message, record update, recommendation or workflow action.

sections.heading: Why now: AI adoption is moving faster than operational control
sections.body: Corentis sits at the intersection of two trends: rising regulated-service pressure and rapid AI/agentic AI adoption. The opportunity is not simply to add more AI, but to make AI actions controlled, reviewable and evidenced.

sections.heading: Why policies alone are insufficient
sections.body: Policies describe intent, but they do not automatically control runtime AI behaviour. Corentis is designed to create a checkpoint before action, so proposed outputs can be checked, routed, paused or recorded before they affect customers, teams or live systems.

sections.heading: Investment thesis
bullets: AI is moving from drafting to acting.
Regulated organisations need control at the action boundary.
Corentis starts with a narrow high-consequence wedge: financial-services complaints and vulnerable-customer workflows.
Complaints and vulnerable-customer workflows are a strong first test because they combine customer harm, regulatory sensitivity, operational pressure and evidence needs.
The reusable assets are control schemas, scenario libraries, evidence artefacts, checkpoint logic and evaluation methods.
The near-term objective is validation, not overclaiming market traction.
The broader opportunity is runtime control infrastructure for regulated AI-agent workflows.

sections.heading: Illustrative human context
sections.body: Example context only: a customer discloses job loss, missed payments and distress after repeated contact. An AI assistant drafts a standard reply. Corentis Shield would test whether that output should pause, route to human review and record evidence before any customer communication proceeds.

sections.heading: The Corentis answer
bullets: Corentis Shield: the checkpoint before AI outputs become real-world action.
Policy Controls: operational rules derived from policy, risk and evidence requirements.
Review Queue: human approval and escalation where judgement is needed.
Assurance Lab: scenario testing before live use.
Evidence Vault: records of proposals, decisions, reviews and timestamps.
Pilot Report: a structured evidence pack for rollout decisions.

sections.heading: Narrow wedge, broader platform
sections.body: The first wedge is financial-services complaints and vulnerable-customer workflows because they combine high contact volume, sensitive customer context, evidence requirements, escalation rules and reputational risk. Once the control pattern works for one regulated workflow, it can be adapted to other regulated support, casework and AI-agent workflows.

sections.heading: Commercial value
bullets: A clearer path to safer AI rollout through a checkpoint-before-action pattern.
Clearer operational oversight around AI-assisted work.
Reusable evidence instead of manual evidence chasing.
Less duplicated governance effort across pilot workflows if validation supports the pattern.
Stronger procurement and buyer confidence through evidence-led controls.

sections.heading: 12-month proof plan
bullets: Make V2 funding pack and resource layer live.
Submit or prepare Frontier AI Discovery-style R&D route.
Run baseline vs checkpointed workflow tests.
Convert evidence into commercial pilot path.

sections.heading: What funding would unlock
bullets: Go-to-market credibility based on measured evidence.

sections.heading: What Corentis is seeking
bullets: Design partners with sensitive regulated workflows.
Pilot conversations around complaints, vulnerability and regulated support.
Strategic investors and funding partners who understand AI assurance infrastructure.
Regulated workflow collaborators who want a practical path from review to deployment.

title: Assurance & Governance Summary PDF
subtitle: How Corentis supports controlled AI workflows and evidence-led assurance.
shortDescription: How Corentis maps AI outputs to controls, review and evidence.
evidenceHook: Shows why assurance needs evidence captured while the workflow runs.
ctaText: Review the sample pilot report or discuss an assurance workflow.
disclaimer: Corentis supports governance and assurance workflows. It does not replace legal, compliance, risk or regulatory judgement. This document is for discussion and pilot exploration only.

sections.heading: What Corentis is
sections.body: Corentis Shield is a checkpoint and evidence layer for AI-assisted regulated workflows. It is designed to help teams review sensitive outputs, route exceptions and capture evidence before action.

sections.heading: What it helps control
bullets: Human approval requirements before sensitive action.

sections.heading: Why assurance needs runtime evidence
sections.body: The commercial risk is not only that an AI model may be wrong. It is that a firm may be unable to show which controls operated, why a sensitive action was paused, who reviewed it, and what evidence was captured.

sections.heading: Control loop
bullets: Check AI outputs or actions at runtime.

title: Design Partner Pack PDF
subtitle: A practical path to pilot Corentis Shield on one regulated workflow.
shortDescription: A practical outline for starting with one sensitive workflow.
evidenceHook: Explains why complaints and vulnerable-customer workflows are a strong first pilot wedge.
disclaimer: This document is for discussion and pilot exploration only. A design partner pilot can begin as non-production testing and should be scoped with appropriate risk, compliance and operational stakeholders.

sections.heading: Who this is for
sections.body: This pack is for regulated teams exploring AI-assisted workflows where actions need controls, review and evidence. The first pilot can be narrow, practical and non-production.

sections.heading: Why start with complaints and vulnerable-customer workflows?
sections.body: A non-production pilot can test whether AI-assisted workflows can be made more controlled before deployment: sensitive outputs paused, vulnerability signals escalated, human approval routed, and evidence captured.

sections.heading: Illustrative human context
sections.body: Example context only: a customer discloses job loss, missed payments and distress after repeated contact. An AI assistant drafts a standard reply. Corentis Shield would test whether that output should pause, route to human review and record evidence before any customer communication proceeds.

sections.heading: What a pilot could test
bullets: Can policies be translated into operational controls?
Can risky AI outputs or actions be caught before action?
Can human review be routed cleanly?
Can the organisation see a clearer go/no-go path?

sections.heading: What Corentis would need
bullets: Feedback on usability and evidence needs.

title: Sample Pilot Report PDF
subtitle: Financial services complaints workflow. Demo / illustrative report.
shortDescription: A report structure for evidence-led controlled AI workflow pilots.
evidenceHook: Shows how evidence could be presented for a fictional vulnerable-customer complaints workflow.
disclaimer: This is fictional demo material. It does not describe a real customer, a real bank, a real complaint or a completed deployment. It is for discussion and pilot exploration only.

sections.heading: Executive summary
sections.body: This illustrative report shows how Corentis Shield could support a controlled pilot for a financial-services complaints assistant focused on vulnerable-customer handling. AI can assist with triage, drafting and recommendations. Sensitive customer messages and final decisions require checkpointing and human review.

sections.heading: Why this sample workflow matters
sections.body: Complaints, hardship and vulnerable-customer workflows combine customer emotion, operational pressure, evidence requirements and reputational risk. They are a practical first place to test whether AI-assisted work can be made more controlled before deployment.

sections.heading: Use-case boundary
bullets: AI can assist with triage, drafting and recommendations.
AI cannot directly send sensitive customer messages without human review.
AI cannot make final complaint decisions without evidence checks.
Hardship, vulnerability and complaint-risk signals should trigger review or escalation.

sections.heading: Control objectives
bullets: Require human approval for sensitive communications.
Capture evidence for decisions and review.

sections.heading: Illustrative human context
sections.body: Example context only: a customer discloses job loss, missed payments and distress after repeated contact. An AI assistant drafts a standard reply. Corentis Shield would test whether that output should pause, route to human review and record evidence before any customer communication proceeds.

sections.heading: Sample pilot dashboard
bullets: Workflow boundary: complaints assistant for triage, drafting and recommendations only.
Scenario coverage: vulnerability, hardship, repeated contact, unsupported closure and urgent support.
Checkpoint outcomes: proceed, pause, escalate, block or evidence-required.
Review routes: complaint handler, specialist support, supervisor or customer communications reviewer.
Evidence status: proposed output, policy basis, review decision, timestamp and residual risk note.

sections.heading: Scenario tests
bullets: Customer discloses job loss and missed mortgage payment: checkpoint escalates hardship signal and requires human review.
Complaint includes repeated contact after vulnerability disclosure: checkpoint blocks direct closure and asks for evidence review.
AI drafts overconfident compensation wording: checkpoint routes wording to approval and records version.
AI suggests closing a complaint without adequate investigation: checkpoint blocks closure until investigation evidence is attached.
Customer requests urgent support: checkpoint escalates to a specialist queue and records the reason.

sections.heading: Sample scenario table
bullets: Scenario: job loss and missed mortgage payment. Checkpoint outcome: pause standard reply. Review route: hardship/specialist review. Evidence captured: disclosure, draft, escalation reason. Residual risk: reviewer judgement still required. Go/no-go learning: vulnerability controls must be explicit.

Scenario: repeated contact after vulnerability disclosure. Checkpoint outcome: block direct closure. Review route: complaints lead. Evidence captured: contact history and missing evidence flag. Residual risk: policy quality matters. Go/no-go learning: closure controls need investigation evidence.

Scenario: overconfident compensation wording. Checkpoint outcome: route wording to approval. Review route: supervisor. Evidence captured: draft, approved version and reviewer timestamp. Residual risk: wording standards need ownership. Go/no-go learning: sensitive wording should be versioned.

Scenario: AI suggests closing a complaint without adequate investigation. Checkpoint outcome: block closure. Review route: complaints lead / evidence reviewer. Evidence captured: missing investigation flag, complaint history and requested artefacts. Residual risk: reviewer still needs judgement on sufficiency. Go/no-go learning: closure controls need clear evidence requirements.

Scenario: urgent support request. Checkpoint outcome: escalate. Review route: specialist queue. Evidence captured: urgency signal and routing decision. Residual risk: escalation capacity must be planned. Go/no-go learning: review queues need operational ownership.

sections.heading: Control matrix summary
bullets: Risk: vulnerability signal. Control: pause direct response. Evidence: disclosure and review note.
Risk: unsupported closure. Control: block closure. Evidence: investigation history required.
Risk: sensitive compensation wording. Control: review required. Evidence: approved wording/version recorded.

sections.heading: Go/no-go recommendation
sections.body: Proceed to a controlled design-partner pilot. Do not treat this illustrative report as approval for enterprise rollout.

title: Control Matrix Example PDF
subtitle: Illustrative example only.
shortDescription: Example control mapping for policy, risk, review and evidence.
evidenceHook: Maps vulnerable-customer and financial-difficulty pressure into reviewable controls.
ctaText: Discuss how this matrix could map to your workflow.
disclaimer: Illustrative example only. This document does not constitute legal, regulatory or compliance advice.

matrixRows.policyIntent: Handle vulnerability signals carefully and avoid inappropriate pressure.
matrixRows.runtimeCheckpoint: Pause direct response when vulnerability is detected.
matrixRows.humanReviewTrigger: Human review required before customer communication.
matrixRows.evidenceCaptured: Disclosure, proposed response, policy version and review decision.
matrixRows.successMeasure: Vulnerability signal is escalated before customer-facing action.

matrixRows.policyIntent: Escalate hardship signals and consider tailored support.
matrixRows.runtimeCheckpoint: Route hardship cases to specialist review.
matrixRows.humanReviewTrigger: Specialist approval before collections or closure action.
matrixRows.evidenceCaptured: Hardship signal, missed-payment context and support review note.
matrixRows.successMeasure: Financial difficulty signal is routed to appropriate review with support context.

matrixRows.policyIntent: Do not close complaints without adequate investigation evidence.
matrixRows.runtimeCheckpoint: Block closure if investigation evidence is missing.
matrixRows.evidenceCaptured: Complaint history, missing evidence flag and closure decision.
matrixRows.successMeasure: Complaint closure does not proceed until evidence requirements are visible.

matrixRows.policyIntent: Ensure compensation or refund wording is accurate and approved.
matrixRows.runtimeCheckpoint: Review required before sensitive wording is sent.
matrixRows.evidenceCaptured: Draft, approved wording/version and reviewer timestamp.
matrixRows.successMeasure: Sensitive wording is approved and versioned before use.

matrixRows.policyIntent: Require enough evidence before complaint outcome or next action.
matrixRows.runtimeCheckpoint: Flag missing investigation artefacts before closure or recommendation.
matrixRows.evidenceCaptured: Missing evidence flag, requested artefacts and review decision.
matrixRows.successMeasure: Reviewer can see what evidence is missing and what must be supplied.

matrixRows.policyIntent: Present information in a way customers can understand.
matrixRows.runtimeCheckpoint: Plain-language review for complex or sensitive messages.
matrixRows.humanReviewTrigger: Review required if communication is unclear or too technical.
matrixRows.evidenceCaptured: Original draft, revised wording and review decision.
matrixRows.successMeasure: Customer-facing communication is reviewed for clarity before sending.

sections.heading: Example control areas grounded in real regulatory pressure
sections.body: The example matrix shows how policy intent can become operational checks, approval points and evidence logs. It is not a template for legal compliance; it is a practical way to make review points visible in a pilot.

sections.heading: Illustrative human context
sections.body: Example context only: a customer discloses job loss, missed payments and distress after repeated contact. An AI assistant drafts a standard reply. Corentis Shield would test whether that output should pause, route to human review and record evidence before any customer communication proceeds.

title: Runtime Checkpoint Explainer PDF
subtitle: A plain-English guide to the checkpoint before action.
shortDescription: A plain-English explanation of the checkpoint before action.
evidenceHook: Explains why checkpoints matter as AI agents move from drafting to acting.
disclaimer: This document is for discussion and pilot exploration only. Corentis Shield is designed to support controlled AI workflows; it does not solve every AI governance issue.

sections.heading: What a runtime checkpoint is
sections.body: A runtime checkpoint is the moment where Corentis checks what an AI system is about to say or do before that action moves forward.

sections.heading: Why checkpoints matter as AI agents move from drafting to acting
sections.body: A checkpoint is the practical control point between an AI-generated recommendation and a sensitive real-world action.

sections.heading: The difference between documents, outputs and checkpoints
bullets: A policy document describes expected behaviour.
An AI model output is a draft reply, recommendation or proposed action.
A runtime checkpoint checks the output before action.
Human review adds judgement where risk, context or evidence requires it.
An evidence log records what was proposed, checked, decided and reviewed.

sections.heading: Simple flow
sections.body: AI proposes -> checkpoint -> proceed / pause / escalate / block -> human review -> evidence.

sections.heading: Why post-event monitoring is not enough
sections.body: Post-event monitoring can help teams learn after something has happened, but it does not by itself decide whether a sensitive AI-generated action should move forward. The checkpoint idea is to make the review point visible before the action affects a customer, case or regulated workflow.

sections.heading: Examples
bullets: AI detects a vulnerable customer signal.
AI tries to close a case without enough evidence.

sections.heading: Why this matters as agents move from drafting to acting
sections.body: When AI only drafts, the main question is whether the text is useful. When AI starts recommending or triggering workflow actions, the question becomes whether the action is controlled, reviewable and evidenced before it moves forward.

sections.heading: Why this matters
bullets: Less blind trust in AI outputs.

sections.heading: What Corentis is
sections.body: Corentis Shield is an AI checkpoint for regulated workflows. It sits between AI agents and regulated actions, checking proposed outputs against policy, risk rules, human-review requirements and evidence requirements before anything sensitive moves forward.

Corentis is positioned around operational assurance: checkpoints, human review, evidence and pilot reports.

sections.heading: Partner with us

sections.heading: Illustrative human context
sections.body: Illustrative context: a customer discloses job loss, missed payments and distress after repeated contact. An AI assistant drafts a standard response. Corentis Shield would test whether that output should pause, route to human review and record evidence before any customer communication proceeds.

sections.heading: Why the problem matters now
sections.body: AI is moving from drafting to acting. Regulated teams need evidence-led controls at the action boundary, not only policy documents or post-event monitoring.

sections.heading: Funding story in one line
sections.body: Corentis is not seeking funding for another generic AI interface. The funding story is the validation of a reusable runtime control pattern: policy intent becomes operational checkpoints, sensitive AI actions pause, human review is routed and evidence is captured as the workflow runs.

sections.heading: What funding would help prove
bullets: Whether policy expectations can become visible runtime controls.
Whether risky AI outputs can be paused before sensitive action.
Whether vulnerable-customer signals can be escalated consistently.
Whether human review can be routed with clear evidence.
Whether pilot reports can support go/no-go decisions.
Whether benchmark scenarios can reveal the difference between uncheckpointed and checkpointed AI workflows.

Sample pilot report and control matrix artefacts.

Days 31-60: prepare or submit the strongest eligible route and start strategic conversations.
Days 61-90: narrow the pilot boundary, scenario library and baseline-versus-checkpoint test plan.
12-month objective: move from prepared materials to non-production validation, evidence scoring and a controlled pilot route if the evidence supports it.

title: ControlBench R&D Brief PDF
subtitle: A feasibility-study framing for runtime assurance and benchmark testing.
shortDescription: A Frontier AI Discovery-style technical R&D brief.
evidenceHook: Frames Corentis around R&D validation, baseline comparison, benchmark testing and evidence completeness.
ctaText: Use this brief to prepare the Corentis ControlBench application narrative.
disclaimer: This is a feasibility concept, not a guarantee of technical success or funding outcome. Eligibility and scope should be confirmed against the live competition rules.

sections.heading: Corentis
sections.body: Corentis ControlBench: runtime assurance and benchmark testing for agentic AI in regulated workflows.

sections.heading: Problem
sections.body: Agentic AI needs measurable runtime assurance before regulated actions. The practical research question is how to evaluate whether a checkpoint improves reviewability, evidence completeness and action safety compared with an unchecked AI-assisted workflow.

sections.heading: Partner with us
sections.body: We are seeking funding, sandbox support and strategic conversations to validate whether runtime checkpointing, policy-to-control mapping, scenario testing and evidence capture can improve the reviewability, control and assurance of AI-assisted regulated workflows.

sections.heading: R&D question
sections.body: Can policy-to-control mapping, runtime checkpoints and scenario-based evaluation improve the safety, reviewability and evidence completeness of agentic AI workflows in regulated contexts?

sections.heading: What funding would help prove
bullets: Whether policy expectations can become visible runtime controls.
Whether risky AI outputs can be paused before sensitive action.
Whether vulnerable-customer signals can be escalated consistently.
Whether human review can be routed with clear evidence.
Whether pilot reports can support go/no-go decisions.
Whether benchmark scenarios can reveal the difference between uncheckpointed and checkpointed AI workflows.

sections.heading: Baseline
sections.body: The baseline is an AI-assisted workflow without the Corentis checkpoint: AI proposes replies, case updates or next actions without a structured runtime control layer.

sections.heading: ControlBench test assets
bullets: A scenario library covering complaints, hardship, repeated contact and vulnerable-customer disclosures.
A control schema that maps policy intent to runtime checkpoint conditions.
A baseline run where AI-assisted outputs are assessed without the Corentis checkpoint.
A checkpointed run where proposed outputs are paused, routed or evidenced before action.
A scoring sheet for evidence completeness, reviewer clarity and escalation quality.

sections.heading: Work packages
bullets: WP1 - Scenario library and control schema. Aim: define realistic regulated workflow scenarios and checkpoint controls. Output: scenario set and control schema. Success measure: scenarios cover vulnerability, hardship, unsupported closure, sensitive wording and missing evidence.

WP2 - Baseline uncheckpointed AI-assisted workflow run. Aim: establish what happens without a runtime checkpoint. Output: baseline outputs and failure-mode notes. Success measure: baseline produces measurable review, escalation and evidence gaps.

WP3 - Checkpointed workflow run. Aim: run the same scenarios with Corentis-style controls. Output: checkpoint decisions, review routes and evidence logs. Success measure: controls visibly allow, pause, route or record proposed actions.

WP4 - Evaluation and scoring. Aim: compare baseline and checkpointed runs. Output: evidence completeness, escalation accuracy, review routing and false positive / false negative analysis. Success measure: scoring is understandable enough for reviewers.

WP5 - Feasibility report and Phase 2 plan. Aim: show what is technically promising and what remains uncertain. Output: feasibility report and next-build plan. Success measure: clear recommendation for whether to proceed and what to validate next.

sections.heading: Key risks and mitigations
bullets: Risk: scenarios are too synthetic. Mitigation: use domain-informed examples and invite expert review before a design partner pilot.
Risk: checkpoint rules are too broad. Mitigation: measure false positives and false negatives rather than assuming perfect control.
Risk: evidence logs are incomplete. Mitigation: define evidence completeness scoring before running tests.
Risk: reviewers do not trust recommendations. Mitigation: include reviewer confidence and clarity measures.
Risk: integration assumptions are unclear. Mitigation: keep feasibility testing bounded and document ownership limits.

sections.heading: Why this is R&D rather than routine product build
sections.body: The uncertain work is not simply making a dashboard. The R&D question is whether runtime checkpointing, policy-to-control mapping and scenario-based evaluation can produce better reviewability and evidence completeness than an uncheckpointed AI-assisted workflow, and whether that can become a repeatable assurance pattern.

sections.heading: Future Phase 2 potential
sections.body: A successful feasibility study could support a larger R&D route around reusable evaluation assets, scenario libraries and runtime assurance infrastructure.

sections.heading: What would count as useful progress
bullets: A repeatable test harness for regulated workflow scenarios.
Defined failure modes for missed escalation, over-escalation and incomplete evidence.
A technical feasibility report showing what to build next and what remains unresolved.

sections.heading: Example scenario families
bullets: A vulnerable-customer disclosure where the AI drafts a standard complaint response without recognising the need for escalation.
A financial difficulty signal where the AI suggests a next action before support options have been reviewed.
A complaint closure attempt where investigation evidence is incomplete or unsupported.
A sensitive compensation wording case where the proposed language requires human approval and version tracking.
An unclear communication case where plain-language review is needed before customer-facing action.

sections.heading: Technical outputs
sections.body: The feasibility study should produce a small but credible technical asset set: a control schema, a scenario test set, baseline and checkpoint comparison results, an evidence completeness scoring method and a clear list of unresolved technical questions for a future Phase 2 build.

title: FCA Supercharged Sandbox Preparation Brief PDF
subtitle: A preparation brief for financial-services AI checkpoint testing.
shortDescription: A sandbox preparation brief for AI-assisted complaints and vulnerable-customer workflows.
evidenceHook: Positions Corentis around financial-services workflow testing without implying FCA approval.
ctaText: Prepare the sandbox use-case statement and supporting pack.
disclaimer: This brief does not imply FCA approval, acceptance or endorsement. It is a preparation document for a sandbox/support route.

sections.heading: Route status
sections.body: The FCA Supercharged Sandbox is scheduled to open on 5 May 2026 and close on 1 June 2026, with outcomes expected on 26 June 2026.

sections.heading: Corentis fit
sections.body: Corentis fits as a financial-services testing concept for complaints, vulnerable-customer handling, AI-assisted casework and customer interaction/service agent controls.

sections.heading: Partner with us
sections.body: We are seeking funding, sandbox support and strategic conversations to validate whether runtime checkpointing, policy-to-control mapping, scenario testing and evidence capture can improve the reviewability, control and assurance of AI-assisted regulated workflows.

sections.heading: Proposed test use case
sections.body: AI-assisted complaints and vulnerable-customer workflow: proposed replies, case updates and next actions are checked before sensitive action.

sections.heading: What sandbox support would help prove
bullets: Whether policy expectations can become visible runtime controls.
Whether risky AI outputs can be paused before sensitive action.
Whether vulnerable-customer signals can be escalated consistently.
Whether human review can be routed with clear evidence.
Whether pilot reports can support go/no-go decisions.
Whether benchmark scenarios can reveal the difference between uncheckpointed and checkpointed AI workflows.

sections.heading: Proposed sandbox testing objective
sections.body: Test whether Corentis Shield can provide a practical runtime checkpoint for AI-assisted complaints and vulnerable-customer workflows in a non-production setting: risky outputs paused, human review routed and evidence captured before any customer communication proceeds.

sections.heading: Testing boundaries
bullets: Non-production test framing unless explicitly approved through the relevant process.
No FCA approval, endorsement or regulatory sign-off implied.
Focus on complaints, vulnerable-customer signals and sensitive customer communications.
Human review remains part of the test design.

sections.heading: What would not be tested
bullets: No live customer communications unless separately approved.
No replacement of compliance, legal, risk or operational judgement.
No claim that the system prevents regulatory penalties or redress.

sections.heading: Example sandbox scenario
sections.body: A customer complaint includes a hardship disclosure, repeated contact history and a request for urgent support. The AI can assist with triage and drafting, but Corentis would test whether sensitive wording, closure recommendations and escalation decisions are paused for human review before action.

sections.heading: Illustrative human context
sections.body: Example context only: a customer discloses job loss, missed payments and distress after repeated contact. An AI assistant drafts a standard reply. Corentis Shield would test whether that output should pause, route to human review and record evidence before any customer communication proceeds.

sections.heading: Evidence produced by the test
bullets: Scenario test log showing what was proposed and how controls responded.
Blocked-action log for unsupported closure or sensitive wording.
Review queue summary showing which cases required human judgement.
Policy version trail connecting controls to the policy basis used during the test.
Pilot report summarising outcomes, residual risks and next-step recommendations.

sections.heading: Why sandbox support matters
sections.body: Sandbox support could provide sector context, testing environment access and relevant tooling support where applicable. Corentis should avoid treating this as cash funding or regulatory approval.

sections.heading: Next action checklist
bullets: Confirm eligibility when the application window opens.
Prepare control matrix and evidence artefact examples.
Use confirmed company details and verify applicant-specific details.
Submit only when the live criteria are verified.

sections.heading: Boundary statement
sections.body: The proposed sandbox preparation is for a controlled test of workflow assurance, not a claim that Corentis has been approved by the FCA or deployed in a live regulated environment.

sections.heading: Why this use case is practical
sections.body: Complaints and vulnerable-customer workflows are a practical first test because they combine customer-impacting communications, policy expectations, escalation needs and evidence requirements. A non-production sandbox-style exercise can test the control pattern without claiming live customer deployment.

sections.heading: Submission evidence to assemble
bullets: A concise use-case description explaining the workflow boundary and why human review is required.
A sample control matrix showing how vulnerable-customer, hardship and unsupported closure signals are handled.
A sample pilot report showing what evidence the checkpoint can produce.
A clear statement that Corentis supports assurance and review workflows rather than replacing compliance judgement.
A list of technical assumptions, integration boundaries and residual risks to validate during testing.

sections.heading: What success would look like
sections.body: A useful sandbox outcome would be a clearer view of which proposed AI actions should continue, pause or escalate; what evidence is needed for oversight; and what technical work is required before any controlled design partner pilot.

sections.heading: Expected learning outcomes
bullets: Which proposed AI actions should pause before customer communication.
Which vulnerability and hardship signals need specialist review.
Which evidence fields are missing from a useful pilot report.
Whether reviewers understand why a checkpoint decision was made.
What integration and ownership questions remain before a design partner pilot.

sections.heading: Materials to bring into the window
bullets: A short note confirming that the exercise is preparation for testing and does not imply regulatory approval.

Whether benchmark scenarios can reveal the difference between uncheckpointed and checkpointed AI workflows.

sections.heading: What is the assurance innovation?
sections.body: The innovation is moving assurance from document-only governance to runtime action-boundary evidence: checking proposed AI outputs before sensitive action, connecting policy intent to visible checkpoint decisions and producing evidence while the workflow runs.

sections.heading: Illustrative human context
sections.body: Example context only: a customer discloses job loss, missed payments and distress after repeated contact. An AI assistant drafts a standard reply. Corentis Shield would test whether that output should pause, route to human review and record evidence before any customer communication proceeds.

sections.heading: Assurance questions to test
bullets: Can a policy expectation be translated into a visible runtime control?
Can the system explain why a proposed AI action was allowed, paused or escalated?
Can evidence be captured while the workflow runs rather than reconstructed afterwards?
Can human reviewers see the policy basis, scenario context and recommended next step?
Can a pilot report make residual risks clear enough for a controlled go/no-go decision?

sections.heading: Pilot concept
sections.body: A regulated complaints/vulnerable-customer workflow where sensitive outputs are paused, reviewed, escalated or recorded before live use.

sections.heading: Monitoring note
sections.body: This pack is intended to keep the assurance narrative ready while the live application route is monitored. Corentis should update deadlines, eligibility and evidence requirements when an official application page is published.

sections.heading: Why runtime assurance matters
sections.body: Governance frameworks and policy documents are important, but they do not automatically show what happened when an AI-assisted workflow generated a customer-impacting recommendation. Corentis is designed to help create that operational evidence at the checkpoint where action is about to happen.

sections.heading: Example validation path
bullets: Select a bounded regulated workflow where AI assists with drafting, triage or recommendations.
Translate a small number of policy expectations into control conditions that can be tested.
Run scenario tests that include vulnerability, hardship, missing evidence and unclear communication.
Compare unchecked AI-assisted outputs against checkpointed outputs and review routes.
Assess whether the resulting evidence artefacts are useful to assurance, risk and operational teams.

sections.heading: Assurance limits
sections.body: The aim is not to replace legal, compliance, risk or regulatory judgement. The aim is to create a clearer operational evidence layer that helps those teams see what controls operated and where human review was required.

sections.heading: Readiness work before launch
bullets: Confirm the live fund scope, eligibility and application deadline when published.
Prepare the assurance summary, control matrix and sample pilot report as supporting materials.
Define a realistic non-production pilot boundary.
Prepare a technical explanation of the runtime checkpoint and evidence capture mechanism.
Keep all claims careful until formal testing produces stronger evidence.

title: Sovereign AI Strategic Memo PDF
subtitle: UK control infrastructure for regulated AI-agent deployment.
shortDescription: A strategic memo for Sovereign AI-style investor/funding outreach.
evidenceHook: Positions Corentis as reusable UK AI control infrastructure rather than a single workflow tool.
ctaText: Use this memo for strategic investor/funding conversations.
disclaimer: This is an investor-style strategic memo. It does not claim Sovereign AI funding, acceptance or endorsement.

sections.heading: Strategic framing
sections.body: Corentis is building UK control infrastructure for regulated AI agents: a runtime checkpoint layer that helps teams control proposed AI outputs before sensitive actions move forward.

sections.heading: Why this is bigger than one workflow
sections.body: The first workflow proves the pattern, but the reusable assets are control logic, scenario libraries, evidence artefacts and checkpoint methods.

sections.heading: Strategic asset logic
sections.body: If the pattern works, Corentis can become infrastructure for repeated regulated AI deployment decisions: define the boundary, translate policy into controls, test scenarios, checkpoint proposed actions, route human review and produce evidence for assurance.

sections.heading: Partner with us
sections.body: We are seeking funding, sandbox support and strategic conversations to validate whether runtime checkpointing, policy-to-control mapping, scenario testing and evidence capture can improve the reviewability, control and assurance of AI-assisted regulated workflows.

sections.heading: Why this could matter for UK AI capability
bullets: The UK does not only need access to AI models; it also needs control infrastructure for safe deployment in regulated workflows.
Regulated AI adoption will depend on trust, evidence and operational assurance.
Runtime checkpointing could become a reusable layer between AI agents and sensitive actions.
Scenario libraries, control schemas and evidence artefacts could become reusable UK AI assurance assets.
Corentis starts with financial services because the use case is concrete, high-consequence and evidence-heavy.
The broader pattern may transfer to other regulated workflows.

sections.heading: What strategic support would help prove
bullets: Whether policy expectations can become visible runtime controls.
Whether risky AI outputs can be paused before sensitive action.
Whether vulnerable-customer signals can be escalated consistently.
Whether human review can be routed with clear evidence.
Whether pilot reports can support go/no-go decisions.
Whether benchmark scenarios can reveal the difference between uncheckpointed and checkpointed AI workflows.

sections.heading: Why now
sections.body: AI is moving from drafting to acting. Regulated teams need infrastructure at the action boundary, where review, escalation and evidence can happen before real-world impact.

sections.heading: Narrow wedge
sections.body: Financial services complaints and vulnerable-customer workflows provide a concrete first test because they are sensitive, evidence-heavy and operationally relevant.

sections.heading: Broader expansion
bullets: Public services where review and evidence matter.

sections.heading: What early support would unlock
bullets: A sharper technical evaluation route for runtime control and evidence completeness.
A stronger design partner pilot package for regulated workflow testing.
More robust scenario libraries and control patterns.
Better investor and procurement evidence without claiming live deployment.
A clearer path from early prototype to tested pilot-ready infrastructure.

sections.heading: What support would unlock
bullets: Compute and testing support where relevant.
Stronger route to UK-based AI assurance capability.

sections.heading: Careful stage statement
sections.body: Corentis is early-stage. The immediate objective is to validate the control pattern, not to claim market adoption, regulatory approval or completed enterprise deployment.

sections.heading: Why Sovereign AI-style support could matter
sections.body: Strategic support could help Corentis build technical depth around runtime assurance, scenario testing, evidence artefacts and reusable checkpoint patterns. That support would be most useful if connected to real validation work, not a broad marketing push.

sections.heading: Signals to build before a deeper conversation
bullets: A live V2 website with clear audience journeys and downloadable evidence packs.
A ControlBench R&D brief showing the technical validation route.
A sandbox preparation brief for financial-services workflow testing.
A design partner narrative that starts with a narrow non-production pilot.
A concise explanation of how the runtime checkpoint differs from a chatbot, dashboard or policy document.

sections.heading: Strategic diligence questions
bullets: Can the control pattern transfer beyond the first complaints workflow?
Can evidence artefacts be standardised enough to support repeatable assurance?
Can scenario libraries become a defensible technical asset?
Can Corentis integrate at the action boundary without owning the whole workflow system?
Can the company show careful progress without overclaiming regulatory status or customer traction?

sections.heading: Near-term ask
sections.body: The immediate ask is a strategic conversation about whether Corentis should be developed as UK AI control infrastructure, which validation work matters most and which funding, compute or design partner route would create the strongest evidence without premature commercial claims.

sections.heading: Near-term strategic ask
bullets: Conversation with Sovereign AI or aligned strategic funding partners.
Potential support route if Corentis can prove the control pattern.

title: 90-Day Funding Execution Plan PDF
subtitle: Founder execution roadmap for funding and sandbox readiness.
shortDescription: A practical route from V2 launch to applications, strategic outreach and design partner preparation.
evidenceHook: Shows the execution order without claiming any application has been submitted or awarded.
ctaText: Use this plan to sequence the next funding and partnership actions.
disclaimer: This roadmap is preparatory. It does not state that any application has been submitted, accepted or awarded.

sections.heading: Purpose
sections.body: This is a founder execution roadmap. It is not evidence that funding has been secured, submitted or accepted.

sections.heading: Owner/action/checkpoint plan
bullets: Founder owner: prepare application-specific details. Action: use confirmed company details, then confirm applicant eligibility, project budget and dates. Checkpoint: details ready before any formal submission.
Founder owner: prepare ControlBench route. Action: refine R&D question, work packages and validation metrics. Checkpoint: Frontier AI Discovery-style pack ready for review.
Founder owner: prepare sandbox route. Action: define non-production complaints/vulnerable-customer testing boundary. Checkpoint: sandbox use-case statement ready.
Founder owner: prepare strategic memo route. Action: use Sovereign AI memo for careful strategic conversations. Checkpoint: feedback captured without claiming support.
Founder owner: prepare design partner route. Action: build first manual target list and pilot conversation structure. Checkpoint: first conversations logged.

sections.heading: First 30 days
bullets: Review all PDFs for accuracy and tone.
Use confirmed company details and confirm application-specific details.
Build the first design partner and strategic funding target list.

sections.heading: Days 31-60
bullets: Submit or prepare the most urgent R&D route depending on live eligibility.
Open strategic conversations using the Sovereign AI memo.
Refine scenario library and control schema.
Capture feedback on evidence artefacts and workflow boundaries.

sections.heading: Days 61-90
bullets: Move from preparation to validation planning.
Select the strongest non-production pilot scenario.
Decide whether to proceed, pause or narrow the next funding route.

sections.heading: Phase 1: make V2 live and test
sections.body: Confirm the website, PDFs, contact flow, sitemap, robots and social preview work before outreach.

sections.heading: Phase 2: prepare Frontier AI Discovery
sections.body: Frame Corentis ControlBench as a feasibility study around runtime assurance, benchmark testing and evidence completeness.

sections.heading: Phase 2 outputs
bullets: Budget and eligibility details to confirm manually.

sections.heading: Phase 3: send Sovereign AI strategic approach
sections.body: Use the strategic memo to approach this as investor-style infrastructure funding, not a standard grant.

sections.heading: Phase 4: prepare FCA Supercharged Sandbox
sections.body: Prepare the sandbox application pack for the 5 May 2026 opening window and avoid implying FCA approval.

sections.heading: Phase 4 outputs
bullets: Use-case statement for AI-assisted complaints and vulnerable-customer workflows.
Assurance summary and runtime checkpoint explainer.
Company details and eligibility information confirmed before submission.

sections.heading: Phase 5: monitor AI Assurance Innovation Fund
sections.body: Keep the readiness brief prepared and monitor for a verified live application page.

sections.heading: Phase 6: partner-check benchmarking dataset route
sections.body: Only pursue the Frontier AI Benchmarking Datasets route if a realistic consortium or partner route emerges.

sections.heading: Phase 7: consider NPIF II later
sections.body: Consider regional finance after first external signals, application progress or design partner conversations.

sections.heading: Decision gates
bullets: Gate 1: route eligibility confirmed before writing a full application.
Gate 2: ControlBench R&D question reviewed before submission.
Gate 3: sandbox boundary confirmed before any live-testing language is used.
Gate 4: design partner conversations show enough interest to justify deeper pilot scoping.
Gate 5: evidence and reviewer feedback are strong enough to support the next commercial route.

sections.heading: What to stop doing if it distracts from funding
bullets: Avoid broad product expansion before the first validation route is clear.
Avoid chasing generic AI messaging that weakens the checkpoint/infrastructure story.
Avoid overbuilding public pages when the immediate need is application evidence.
Avoid large cold outreach batches before early responses are reviewed.
Avoid claims that sound stronger than the validation evidence.
